content.problemStatement:
You are given two binary strings AAA and BBB with the same length.

You may perform the following operation any number of times (including zero): pick a substring of AAA with odd length and invert all the bits (change '0' to '1' or vice versa) at odd positions in this substring. For example, if we choose a substring "**0**1**0**1**1**", we can convert it to "**1**1**1**1**0**" (bits at odd positions are bold).

Determine the minimum number of operations required to change the string AAA to the string BBB.
constraints: The sum of |A| over all test cases does not exceed 10^5
A and B consist only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, two lines follow: the first line is the binary string A, the second line is the binary string B. Both strings have the same length.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of operations required to transform A into B.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: mismatches at positions 2,4 (even) form one group and position 5 (odd) forms another → 2 operations.\nCase 2: strings are identical → 0 operations.\nCase 3: mismatches at positions 1 and 3 (odd) are consecutive in odd index list → one odd group, position 2 (even) → one even group, total 2.
Single position (odd) mismatches, forming one group → 1 operation.
All odd positions (1,3,5) mismatched → one odd group. All even positions (2,4) mismatched → one even group. Total 2.

solution.approach:
Observe that an operation chooses a substring of odd length and flips bits at positions having the same parity as the left end of the substring. Consequently, the operation flips a contiguous block of indices that share the same global parity (all odd positions or all even positions). For each parity we can treat mismatched positions (where A[i] != B[i]) as 1 and matches as 0. Flipping a contiguous block toggles all bits inside it, so the optimal way is to flip each maximal consecutive segment of 1s exactly once. Therefore the answer equals the number of groups of mismatches among odd indices plus the number of groups among even indices. The algorithm scans the strings once, maintaining two flags that indicate whether we are currently inside a mismatch group for odd and even positions respectively, and increments the corresponding counters when a new group starts.

The procedure is O(N) per test case and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the strings. The total over all test cases is O( Σ N ) ≤ 10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input strings.
